rationale: Authority data for W40K-0061..W40K-0070 — the Iron Warriors trio that closes Graham McNeill's chaos-side material (the 2012 Omnibus, the 2019 Complete Honsou Omnibus, and the 2010 limited-edition Iron Warrior novella), followed by six early-2000s 40K standalones whose lore has aged into Black Library's Heretic Tomes label: Ian Watson's foundational Astartes Bildungsroman Space Marine (1993, Imperial Fists from Necromunda underhive recruits), Barrington J. Bayley's surreal British-New-Wave Eye of Terror (1999, Rogue Trader + corrupted-Marine subplot inside the Eye), Brian Craig/Stableford's chaos-cult character study Pawns of Chaos (2001, medieval Sigmatus), William King's rogue-trader-meets-Farseer journey Farseer (2002), Ben Counter's baroque chaos-world spectacle Daemon World (2003, Torvendis), Gav Thorpe's foundational Dark Angels / Fallen-Angels interrogation Angels of Darkness (2003, Astelan + Piscina IV), and Simon Spurrier's rare Tau-POV game-tie-in Fire Warrior (2003, Kais on Dolumar IV). Two repackaging-pair flags (W40K-0061 ≡ W40K-0062, both Iron Warriors omnibuses with identical eight constituents) and four canonicity_drift flags (Heretic Tomes label) are the structural notes for this batch. First explicit Word Bearers appearance, first Tau-POV-anchored entry, and first Eldar-Farseer-POV-supporting entry in the authority layer.

overrides.synopsis: Graham McNeill's first omnibus of Iron Warriors stories, all anchored on the warsmith Honsou. The novel Storm of Iron runs the methodical siege of the Imperial fortress-world Hydra Cordatus, with Warsmith Barban Falk and the captains Forrix, Kroeger and a rising Honsou tearing apart wall after wall to reach the primarch-DNA vault buried beneath. The novella Iron Warrior delivers Honsou's near-impossible vendetta strike on an Ultramarines star fort, and six bridging shorts thread his career through the Eye of Terror, the Skull Harvest and the long Ventris obsession. Strictly chaos-POV — the legion seen from inside, hubris and workmanship intact together.

sources: Black Library product listings; Lexicanum Iron Warriors (Novel Series); Goodreads
flags.reasoning: Content-identical to W40K-0062 Iron Warriors: The Complete Honsou Omnibus (2019). Both collect the same eight constituents: the novel Storm of Iron, the novella Iron Warrior, and the six shorts The Enemy of My Enemy, The Heraclitus Effect, The Skull Harvest, The Iron Without, The Beast of Calth, and The Corpse Road. Roster carries both rows because they exist as distinct SKUs (different ISBNs, different cover art, seven-year publication gap). Resolver should treat them as the same logical work for recommendation purposes but keep separate book rows for catalogue completeness.

overrides.synopsis: The 2019 repackaging of Graham McNeill's first Iron Warriors omnibus under the Complete Honsou Omnibus title — same eight constituents, same chaos-POV across the warsmith Honsou's career. Storm of Iron runs the siege of the Imperial fortress-world Hydra Cordatus under Warsmith Barban Falk, with Forrix, Kroeger and a rising Honsou tearing apart its walls to reach Magos Naicin's primarch-DNA vault. The novella Iron Warrior delivers Honsou's revenge strike on an Ultramarines star fort, and six bridging shorts thread his arc through the Eye of Terror, the Skull Harvest and the Ventris obsession. Same eight pieces under a different cover and seven extra years of cover art — same Honsou, same workmanship, same long appetite for treason.

sources: Black Library product listings; Lexicanum Iron Warriors (Novel Series); Kobo / Apple Books / Amazon listings
flags.reasoning: Content-identical to W40K-0061 Iron Warriors: The Omnibus (2012). Both collect the same eight constituents. Roster carries both rows because they exist as distinct SKUs (different ISBNs, different cover art, seven-year publication gap). Resolver should treat them as the same logical work for recommendation purposes but keep separate book rows for catalogue completeness.

overrides.synopsis: Warsmith Honsou's hatred of Uriel Ventris drives him to attempt the nigh-impossible: capture one of the most heavily defended star forts in the Ultramarines' holdings, an installation that conceals a secret deep at its core. The novella runs the assault strictly from the Iron Warriors' side, Honsou's hand-picked warband against interlocking fortress firepower, and tilts the saga's gravity decisively to chaos-POV. The Ventris obsession is by this point a sustained way of life rather than a phase, and the novella is its rawest articulation. A fast siege-piece carved from a long grudge.

Three boys from the underhive gangs of Hive Trazior on Necromunda — Lexandro d'Arquebus, Yeremi Valance and Biff Tundrish — are dragged into the Imperial Fists' induction programme. The novel follows their gene-seed implantation, scout trials and ascension to full battle-brother status, closing on a First Company boarding action inside a Tyranid bioship. A Bildungsroman in three POVs — Lexandro's ascetic discipline, Yeremi's intuitive aggression, Biff's bawdy loyalty — set against the chapter's iron Codex orthodoxy. The third Warhammer 40,000 novel ever published (Games Workshop, 1993), and the structural template for every later path-to-Astartes story.

sources: Black Library / Lexicanum: novel sold under the Heretic Tomes brand
flags.reasoning: Black Library currently distributes the novel under its *Heretic Tomes* label, reserved for works no longer consistent with current Warhammer 40,000 canon. The 1993 lore around Imperial Fists recruitment, gene-seed rites, and the now-removed Squat sub-faction has been retconned. Roster correctly lists the book as a 40K novel; this flag is informational for the recommender so it can de-weight or contextualise canonicity-drift entries in Ask-the-Archive results.

overrides.synopsis: On the eve of a new Black Crusade, the Rogue Trader Maynard Rugolo runs a lighter-than-lighter cargo into the fringes of the Eye of Terror, calculating that the warp-twisted reefs between Imperial space and Abaddon's pulpits hold both fortunes and short paths home. A parallel thread follows two Space Marines whose fates entwine deep inside the Eye — one a Khornate corsair of long standing, the other a survivor from the time of the Great Crusade whose loyalist faith is being eroded by everything around him, until he must choose between scorning the Emperor or throwing his lot in with the dark gods his warband-brother already serves. Subplots: a Navigator losing his mind under the Eye's constantly-shifting topography; frog-people brokering diamonds with a Chaos merchant; Marine-on-Marine cannibalism; and two Greater Daemons playing a literal game of pieces-on-a-board by smashing two real planets together. Bayley's prose sits closer to British New-Wave SF than to standard 40K — philosophical, surreal, unsettling — and the book is now under Black Library's Heretic Tomes label for cumulative lore drift since 1999.

sources: Black Library / Lexicanum: novel sold under the Heretic Tomes brand
flags.reasoning: Black Library distributes this 1999 Bayley novel under its *Heretic Tomes* label for works no longer consistent with current 40K canon. Roster correctly lists it as a 40K novel; flag is informational for the recommender.

flags.reason: Beyond Rogue Trader Maynard Rugolo (the named POV), the two Space Marine subplot protagonists and the Navigator who goes mad inside the Eye are described in summary across review sources but their personal names are not consistently surfaced; deliberately left unenumerated rather than invented.

overrides.synopsis: On the medieval-tech world of Sigmatus, isolated for generations inside a warp storm and slowly converted into a tributary of the Imperium, an indigenous rebel cult prepares a daemonic summoning to throw the Adeptus Arbites garrison and the thin Imperial Guard occupation force off their soil. The novel is told largely from the cultist side: the rebels are not monsters but human beings worn flat by Imperial exploitation, philosophically reasoning their way toward a pact with the warp. Their summoning succeeds and a Daemon of Chaos comes through, with all the cost such bargains carry. "Brian Craig" is a Black-Library pseudonym for the British SF author Brian Stableford; the prose is his usual existential-rumination register, not the standard 40K battle-pulp, and the book now sits under Heretic Tomes for the cumulative canonicity drift that catches several early-period 40K standalones written by SF generalists.

sources: Lexicanum / Fandom: novel listed under Heretic Tomes for canonicity drift
flags.reasoning: Distributed under Black Library's *Heretic Tomes* label, reserved for works no longer consistent with current 40K canon. Roster correctly carries the book; flag is informational. (Also worth noting: "Brian Craig" is the Black-Library pseudonym for the British SF author Brian Stableford — useful for the persons table when the resolver auto-creates the author row.)

flags.reason: Cultist-side ensemble cast with multiple POVs across the rebel cell; individual character names are not consistently surfaced across review sources beyond the broad ensemble framing. Left unenumerated rather than invented.

overrides.synopsis: Rogue Trader Janus Darke, once rich and famous and now skidding toward ruin in a drug-ridden frontier-world dive, finds himself joined at his table by Auric — an alien with cheekbones too sharp for any human gene-line and a manner that bends the room around him. Auric is an Eldar Farseer, and he has a craftworld-scale problem: a Daemon Prince of Slaanesh is hunting Darke's soul, the runes have shown Auric where their paths cross, and only at that intersection can the prince be killed. The novel runs the unlikely alliance — rogue trader, Farseer, and Darke's voidship crew — across a journey into the Eye of Terror, with Inquisition agents converging because no human, even one carrying a Farseer for a passenger, should be inside the Eye even by choice. William King writes the Eldar interior with rare interiority for the early-2000s era; the book is now under Black Library's Heretic Tomes label for the cumulative changes to Eldar canon since 2002.

sources: Black Library / Lexicanum: novel sold under Heretic Tomes brand
flags.reasoning: Distributed under Black Library's *Heretic Tomes* label for cumulative canonicity drift in Eldar lore since 2002 (pre-9th-edition Aeldari spelling, pre-Ynnari craftworld politics, etc.). Roster correctly carries the book; flag is informational. The 2002 rogue-trader-meets-Farseer concept and Slaanesh-Daemon-Prince soul-hunt plot itself remain narratively intact.

overrides.synopsis: Torvendis is a daemon world deep inside the Maelstrom, ruled openly by Lady Charybdia in the name of Slaanesh from a hedonistic mega-city built atop strata of millennia of battle and death. A Word Bearers warband under Captain Amakyre arrives hunting one of their own — the traitor Karnulon, an Astartes who broke from the Legion's faith for purposes of his own. As Amakyre's hunt threads through Charybdia's court, the young tribal warlord Golgoth pushes from the wastes to seize his own holding, and the fugitive sorcerer Kron crosses every faction with motives all his own. Beneath all of them lurks a true horror bound deep in Torvendis's bones, whose escape would unmake the planet and threaten the wider Maelstrom. Strictly chaos-side: no significant Imperial presence on stage, the Violators (Charybdia's pet Heretic Astartes warband) function as her enforcers, and the four-way chaos politics across Slaanesh / Khorne / Tzeentch / Nurgle drift across the book's surface. Ben Counter's most baroque early novel and the loud Slaaneshi entry in the Black Library catalogue.

Two stories run in alternating chapters. In the first, on Piscina IV, a Dark Angels strike force under Master Belial defends an Imperial Guard regiment and a Piscinan PDF against an Ork orbital invasion — a fast-moving conventional-war structure that doubles as the chapter's public face. In the second, the Interrogator-Chaplain Boreas conducts the slow, controlled interrogation of Astelan, one of the Fallen captured in deep-warp transit. Astelan does not deny his identity as a Fallen Angel of the Great Crusade — he argues, calmly and at length, that he served the Lion's true commands and that Luther's later actions misrepresented those commands, that the Lion himself was the traitor and the Fallen the loyalists. Boreas reads the transcript like a noose. Astelan's account is unreliable, deliberately structured to break a young Interrogator-Chaplain's certainties, and the novel's central question is whether the Dark Angels' centuries-old hunt for the Fallen rests on the moral certainty the chapter claims. Foundational text for Fallen-Angels lore.

Shas'la T'au Kais, a Fire-Caste trooper fresh from the trial of fire, drops onto Dolumar IV with a hunter-cadre sent to rescue Aun'el Ko'vash — an Ethereal who has crash-landed behind Imperial lines. A clean extraction tangles into a multi-faction nightmare: Imperial Guard and Astartes hold the surface, a Chaos cult below the city has engineered Ko'vash's capture as bait for the soul-substance to free the Greater Daemon Tarkh'ax from a buried fragment of the Webway, and an Inquisition strike-team converges in pursuit. Kais cuts through every layer of betrayal, his caste-discipline fracturing under the rising kill-count. Tie-in novelisation of THQ's 2003 PlayStation 2 game; a rare Tau-POV anchor in early-2000s Black Library.
